title: Calligraphy border design with a broad-edge nib
subtitle: Building decorative rule lines, vine borders, and geometric frames
excerpt: A calligraphy border uses the same broad-edge nib as the text inside it. Simple borders can be built from ruled lines in varied weights; more elaborate ones use vine scrolls, diamond patterns, and pen-made interlace. The key is planning the border width before ruling the page.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), public domain, Internet Archive, chapters on borders and decoration. Johnston describes rule-line borders, vine borders, and their relationship to the letter height.

glossaryTerms.definition: A calligraphy border made from parallel horizontal and vertical lines drawn with a broad-edge nib and ruler. The simplest form of calligraphic framing.

content.text: A border drawn with the same nib as the text it frames looks as though it belongs on the page; a border drawn with a different tool or at a different weight reads as a separate element. The practical rule: the inner border line is at the weight of the text's minor strokes, and the outer border line is at the weight of the text's major strokes. For a Mitchell size 3 text, the inner line uses a size 6 nib (thinner); the outer line uses a size 0 or size 1 (thicker).

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, size 3 (slug: nib-mitchell-roundhand-3)
items.description: For the main text and mid-weight border lines.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, size 6 (slug: nib-mitchell-roundhand-6)
items.description: For the finest border lines (inner rule lines, vine stems).

items.name: Iron-gall ink (slug: iron-gall-ink)
items.description: Use the same ink for both text and border.

content.text: is the simplest form: a rectangle of ruled lines. Plan it before writing the text. Mark the text area dimensions lightly in pencil, then mark a second rectangle 6 mm outside the first for the inner border and a third rectangle 3 mm outside the second for the outer border. Rule the inner lines with the size 6 nib and the outer lines with the size 3 nib. The corners are simply overlapped; the overlap reads as a corner ornament if kept consistent.

Between the two rule lines, small repeated shapes can be added. A diamond is a square rotated 45° and drawn in two pen strokes: one diagonal down-left and one diagonal down-right, meeting at the foot. At pen angle 30°, the two strokes are at roughly equal weight. Equally spaced diamonds fill the border strip. Spacing: centre each diamond over a nib-width interval; for a size 3 nib, one diamond every 4-5 mm along the border strip.

A vine border uses a continuous S-curve (the vine stem) worked with the size 6 nib, with small leaf or berry shapes added at each curve. Work the vine stem first: a flowing line of connected S-curves running along the border strip, keeping the curves consistent in depth and spacing. Then add leaves: small oval shapes made with two curved strokes at the extremities of each S-curve loop. For a period-appropriate flavour, turn the leaf strokes so the leaf axis points toward the border centre rather than outward.
The most useful planning tool is a pencil sketch of the full border at actual size before picking up a nib. Border design is measurement work first, execution second.
